results.possibleIdentity: Ralph Schwitzgebel (later known as Kirkland R. Gable) was a psychologist, inventor, and researcher active during the 1960s and 1970s, whose work intersected with the fields of technology, social engineering, and the arts. He is primarily known for co-inventing the first electronic monitoring system (the electronic ankle bracelet) in 1964 with his twin brother, Robert Schwitzgebel, at Harvard University. Described by his son as a "tinkerer and artist with technology," Schwitzgebel developed devices for "anthropotelemetry" (the remote measurement of human behavior) to create alternatives to incarceration for juvenile delinquents. His work on behavioral electronics and telemetric systems led to an association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, a non-profit founded by Robert Rauschenberg and Billy Klüver that paired artists with engineers. Schwitzgebel's contributions focused on the use of tracking technology and sensors to monitor and interact with individuals in physical spaces, a theme central to the systems art and interactive installations of the era. His research and collaborative projects were featured in the influential art and science journal 'Leonardo' (e.g., in the early 1970s) and his concepts of 'social gadgeteering' influenced contemporary discussions on surveillance and behavioral art.
